coredata.dc:title: Bandgap-engineered ferroelectric single-crystalline NBT-BT based nanocomposites with excellent visible light-ultrasound catalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Hongyuan
He, Jiahuan
Lu, Xunyu
Wang, Feifei
Guo, Yiping
coredata.dc:description: Bandgap engineered ferroelectrics exhibit encouraging multi-energy catalytic performance by coupling the piezoelectricity and photoexcitation, which shows immense potential for environmental remediation and fuel production. However, it is challenging to prepare nano single-crystalline ferroelectric piezo-photoelectric with strong visible light absorption ability. Here, Ni mediated NBT-BT(NBT-BNT) single-crystalline nanocubes around 100 nm with considerable visible light absorption were synthesized by a high-temperature hydrothermal method. The mechanism of Ni2+ on the formation of NBT-BT nanocubes was proposed. The catalytic efficiency of NBT-BNT nanocubes is enhanced by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The RhB can be degraded within 8 min and the hydrogen production rate reaches up to ∼350 μmol g-1h-1 under visible light-ultrasonic condition. Moreover, under the simulated sunlight-ultrasound condition, RhB can be degraded within merely 3 min and a high H2 production rate of ∼747 μmol g-1h-1 is achieved. This work presents a paradigm for preparing ferroelectric single-crystalline nanocatalysts for multi-energy catalytic application.

The schematic illustration of the NBT-BNT-24 h NCs preparation by high-temperature hydrothermal, in which the mediation of Ni2+ can lead to a two-steps hydrothermal process and can achieve the regulation of NBT-BT based ferroelectrics. (a) The interaction between Ni2+ and the TiO2; (b) The dissolution of reactant. Then Na+, Bi3+, Ba2+ and limited Ti4+ were obtained; (c) The localized adsorbed ions lead to spherical agglomerates; (d) The sufficiently dissolution of TiO2·H2O and then the NBT-BNT NCs are finally obtained.

The catalytic RhB degradation performance by harvesting multisource energies from visible light and ultrasound vibration. (a) RhB degradation results of NBT-BNT NCs under the same conditions. (b) RhB degradation results of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under visible light, ultrasound and visible light-ultrasound condition. (c) The linear curves representing piezo/-photocatalytic dye degradation of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs, which are the experimental data fitted to a first-order kinetic model. (d) The comparison in histograms of catalytic reaction constant for NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs. (e) The cycling performance of RhB degradation under visible light-ultrasound condition. (f) Piezo-photoelectric degradation performance of NBT-BNT NCs@CQDs by the addition of different scavengers for quenching experiment. The error bars are of standard deviation (n = 3).

The catalytic H2 production performance by harvesting multisource energies from visible light and ultrasound vibration. (a–b) The catalytic H2 production results of NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under visible light, ultrasound and visible light-ultrasound conditions. (c) The comparison in histograms for the average hydrogen production rates of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs and NBT-BNT NCs under ultrasound/visible light/visible light-ultrasound conditions. (d) The cycling performance of H2 production by NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under simulated sunlight-ultrasound. (e) The comparison of H2 production rates for typical ferroelectric catalysts under visible light and simulated sunlight-ultrasound conditions. The error bars are of standard deviation (n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bandgap engineered ferroelectrics exhibit encouraging multi-energy catalytic performance by coupling the piezoelectricity and photoexcitation, which shows immense potential for environmental remediation and fuel production. However, it is challenging to prepare nano single-crystalline ferroelectric piezo-photoelectric with strong visible light absorption ability. Here, Ni mediated NBT-BT(NBT-BNT) single-crystalline nanocubes around 100 nm with considerable visible light absorption were synthesized by a high-temperature hydrothermal method. The mechanism of Ni2+ on the formation of NBT-BT nanocubes was proposed. The catalytic efficiency of NBT-BNT nanocubes is enhanced by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The RhB can be degraded within 8 min and the hydrogen production rate reaches up to ∼350 μmol g-1h-1 under visible light-ultrasonic condition. Moreover, under the simulated sunlight-ultrasound condition, RhB can be degraded within merely 3 min and a high H2 production rate of ∼747 μmol g-1h-1 is achieved. This work presents a paradigm for preparing ferroelectric single-crystalline nanocatalysts for multi-energy catalytic application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the overwhelming consumption of fossil-fuel and severe environmental contamination, the development of technologies for alternative clean energy resource and degradation of pollutants are attracting intense research interests. Ferroelectrics have been proved to possess the internal electric fields induced by spontaneous polarization, which can produce the piezoelectric potentials under mechanical stress. The induced potential effectively promotes the separation of charge carriers and drives carriers to the surface of the material to conduct catalytic reaction, which enables the conversion from mechanical/light energy to clean energy. The induced potential effectively promotes the separation of charge carriers. Thus, the multi-energy catalytic properties of these ferroelectrics or the ferroelectric based heterostructures have long been pursued (Yun et al., 2002; Wang, 2007; Fang et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021). Extensive research works on piezo-photoelectric effect for ferroelectrics including PbTiO3, KNbO3, BaTiO3 (Wu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020a), Pb(Zr0.52Ti0.48)O3 (Lin et al., 2014), (Na0.5Bi0.5)TiO3 (Zhang et al., 2020), Bi4Ti3O12 (He et al., 2014) have been carried out. Feng et al. has studied the performance of the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) in details by using lead based PbTiO3 monocrystalline nanoplates, where the catalytic H2 generation rate can be promoted up to 6.5 times by the synergistic effects of ferro-/piezoelectric/photocatalytic effects (Feng et al., 2020). The lead-free ferroelectrics of KNbO3 has exhibited greatly improved piezo-photocatalytic degradation efficiency for organic dye, the piezo-photoelectric degradation rate achieves 4-fold enhancement compared with the photocatalytic degradation rate of KNbO3 (Yu et al., 2019). However, the large bandgap of common ferroelectrics seriously limits their utilization under visible light and near infrared (NIR) light. The demanding need for the full utilization of the solar energy by ferroelectrics has spurred a series of research interests on bandgap modulation, such as KNbO3–BaNbNiO3 (Grinberg et al., 2013), 0.98(K0.5Na0.5)NbO3-0.02BaNbNiO3 (Bai et al., 2017), Mn doped BiFeO3 (Matsuo et al., 2017), Sm–Co co-doped BiFeO3 (Rhaman et al., 2021), plasmonic metal-induced Bi2WO6/TiO2 ternary composite (Kavinkumar et al., 2021) and so on. With introducing a bandgap state or modifying the position of valence band maximum (VBM) and/or conductive band minimum (CBM) through ions doping, the electronics can absorb less energy to leap from valence band to conductive band, getting greater reduction capabilities. In this way, the absorption capability of visible light in the ferroelectrics can be enhanced. Thus, the bandgap engineered ferroelectrics can simultaneously utilize solar and mechanical energy, endowing the multi-energy catalytic ability for renewable energy production and pollutants removal (Huang et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019a; Sakthivel et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a). However, until now, most of the bandgap engineering strategies are based on bulk ferroelectrics. It is still difficult to synthesize bandgap-engineered nanoparticles, let alone the single crystal nanoparticles with regular shape.

Single crystalline nanoparticles have been proved to be highly desirable for improving the catalytic performance. Since the nanoparticles can not only provide large surface area and more available catalytic active sites, but also can be easily deformed to induce larger piezoelectric potential to effectively promote charge separation (Yu et al., 2019). Additionally, the single crystal can provide specific exposing facet to promote the catalytic reaction (Yin et al., 2018; You et al., 2019). However, it is still difficult to use common methods to obtain the bandgap engineered single crystalline nano-ferroelectrics. Therefore, a feasible method for modulating the morphology meanwhile narrowing the bandgap of ferroelectrics is of great importance.

NBT based lead-free ferroelectrics have been regarded as a type of promising photo-piezocatalysts due to the cost-effective synthetic method and outstanding piezoelectric-/ferroelectricity (Jia et al., 2019a; Xiao et al., 2019). In our previous work, we have successfully synthesized the nanosized Ni mediated (Na0.5Bi0.5)TiO3–BaTiO3 (NBT-BNT) through a cost-effective self-propagation method (Xiao et al., 2021), which can realize the utilization of visible and NIR light. However, the rapid annealing process imposes difficulties in the formation of single crystal nanoparticles with uniform size distribution. As a result, there exists a chance for the further improvement in the efficiency of the multi-energy catalytic performance. It is noteworthy that hydrothermal methods are suitable for synthesizing NBT with regular shape and homogenous distribution of particle size (Lu et al., 2013), but the prepared micro cubes are too large for catalytic applications (From 2 μm to 20 μm) (Wang et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2016). Besides, it is challenging to acquire regular NBT-BT nanoparticles through hydrothermal method, indicating that the solitary mediation of BT cannot modulate the morphology of NBT based materials (Shi et al., 2019).

In this work, a facile high-temperature hydrothermal method is adopted to obtain single-crystalline nanocubes of NBT-BNT (NBT-BNT NCs). This intelligent strategy of Ni mediation during hydrothermal process surprisingly realized the simultaneous morphology regulation with specific exposing facet of (100) and visible/near infrared light-absorption ability. The mechanism underlying Ni ion induced morphology evolution is expounded. Furthermore, the CQDs were judiciously loaded on the NBT-BNT NCs to remarkably enhance the visible light utilization efficiency and to promote the separation of carriers. As a result, the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs nanocomposites exhibit ultrahigh piezo-photoelectric degradation rates for different kinds of dye molecules and the hydrogen production rate is increased to 350 μmol g−1h−1 under visible light-ultrasonic condition. Moreover, under the simulated sunlight-ultrasound condition, RhB can be degraded within 3 min and the H2 production rate reaches up to 747 μmol g−1h−1. The ideal scheme of this work can be beneficial for the designing of more structure-regulated nano-ferroelectric catalysts, which are prospective to be employed for clean energy evolution and environment protection.

The schematic diagram for the synthesis of NBT-BNT NCs and the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs is shown in Fig. S1. The nano-sized ferroelectrics of 0.95NBT-0.05BNT (the molar ratio of NBT is 95% and the molar ratio of BNT is 5%) were synthesized through high-temperature hydrothermal (HT-hydrothermal) method. High-purity Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (99.9%, Macklin), Ba(OH)2 (99%, Macklin), Ni(NO3)2 6H2O (99%, Macklin), TiO2 (25 nm, Macklin), NaOH (99.8%, Macklin) were used for the synthesis. For the hydrothermal synthesis, the precursors were weighted according to the stoichiometric ratio and dissolved in 40 ml NaOH solution (16 M). The mixture was then stirred for 6 h at 80 °C a yellow solution was obtained. The prepared mixtures were transferred into 50 ml Teflon lined bottle, sealed into stainless steel autoclave and placed for uniform heating at 260 °C for 12, 18 and 24 h. The samples were named as NBT-BNT-12h, NBT-BNT-18h and NBT-BNT-24h, respectively. For comparison, NBT-BT NPs were synthesized through the same hydrothermal methods. Besides, the NBT-BNT samples were prepared through hydrothermal methods at 200 °C to study the incorporation ability of Ni2+ into the B site of ABO3-type perovskite structure.

CQDs were prepared from graphite oxide (GO) using the similar route of Wang et al. (2011). Firstly, GO was synthesized from the graphite powder according to the method of Hummers. (William S. Hummers, 1958) The prepared GO was added into the HNO3 solution (5 mol/L) and the mixture was refluxed for 12h at 140 °C. After neutralizing and centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was added into the dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut of 1000 Da and dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h. Finally, the transparent solution of CQDs was obtained.

The as-prepared NBT-BNT nanoparticles were further coupled with CQDs by a hydrothermal method to prepare NBT-BNT@CQDs composites. A certain amount of NBT-BNT was dispersed in 40 ml CQDs aqueous solution. The pH of solution was adjusted to 3–4 with the addition of hydrochloric acid. The solution mixture was stirred for 6 h and then transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 6 h. Finally, the green composites were collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm and washed with distilled water and ethanol for three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphology, optical and electrochemistry characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (X'pert PRO, PAN analytical) was used to determine the structure and phase of the prepared samples. The surface morphology was studied by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The microstructures and lattice fringes were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Selected area electron diffraction (SEAD) patterns were used to analyze crystallographic plane (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The light absorption spectrum of NBT-BNT NCs was measured by UV–Vis–NIR (Lambda 750 s) spectrophotometer with the wavelength from 250 nm to 1500 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS UltraDLD*1) measurement was utilized to analyze the chemical state of elements in NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs. Electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of •O2− and •OH radicals trapped by 5,5-dimethy-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) were measured at room temperature on a Bruker A300 spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and PL fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed with a Photon Technology International (PTI) and the laserstrobe fluorescence of the samples were recorded on an LS55 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted with an amplitude of 5 mV at a frequency range from 0.01 to 100 kHz. Piezoresponse force microscope (PFM, MFP-3D, USA), in which a point selected piezoelectric response was studied by applying a bias voltage. Nitrogen adsorption measurements (3FLEX, MICROMERITICS, USA) were carried out at 77 K after the sample was vacuum-degassed for 2 h to remove moisture. The BET method was used to determine the surface area from the adsorption measurement at low relative pressures (0.05 < P/P0 < 0.3).

The photo-/piezo-/piezo-photoelectric activities of the NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs were studied by measuring the catalytic degradation efficiency of RhB, methyl orange (MO) and methylene blue (MB). The dyes solution (10 mg L−1) was prepared by mixing RhB, MO or MB with distilled water and stirring for 24 h. The as-prepared NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs powder (0.1 g) was immersed into the dye solution (50 ml). The light irradiation source was provided by a 300 W Xe lamp source (PLS-SXE300+/UV) and visible light was obtained by applying a 420 nm cutoff filter. The periodic mechanical strain was applied by using an ultrasonic cleaner (Branson 2800) with a power of 200 W and a frequency of 40 kHz. The reaction mixture was stirred magnetically in the dark for 30 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution mixture was then irradiated by the Xe lamp from a distance of 15 cm and/or the ultrasonic treatment. Then 1 ml solution was collected at each time interval and the upper clear layer solution was obtained by centrifuging the solution with 10000 rpm for 5 min. The dyes degradation performance was measured by a UV–Vis spectrometer, in which the main absorption peaks of RhB, MO and MB are at 554 nm, 464 nm and 665 nm, respectively. To explore the mechanism of dyes catalytic degradation efficiency, various scavengers were used, including 0.1 ml triethanolamine (TEOA), 0.1 ml tert-butanol (TBA) and nitrogen flow for the quenching of generated holes(h+), hydroxyl radicals (∙OH) and superoxide anion-radicals ∙O2−, respectively.

For the measurement of H2 production rate, the Pyrex reaction cell connected to a closed gas-circulation system (Labsolar-IIIAG, Beijing Perfectlight Co.Ltd.) were used. 35 mg of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs powders were dispersed in 100 ml of an aqueous solution containing 15 vol% triethanolamine. The solution mixture was vacuumed for 1 h to remove any existing gas from the reactor. The temperature of the catalytic system was maintained at 20 °C by using a recirculating water-cooling system. Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter and ultrasonic cleaner were used as sources for the light irradiation and mechanical stress. The H2 evolution rates were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Techcomp GC-7900) and the produced H2 amounts were recorded at each hour intervals.

The powder XRD patterns of the hydrothermally synthesized NBT-BNT powder samples with different reaction times are shown in Fig. 1 a. XRD patterns of NBT-BNT-12h and NBT-BNT-18h show some impurity peaks mainly consist of TiO2 (PDF#21–1236) and BaTiO3 (PDF#05–0626). This justifies the abundance of unreacted TiO2 left in these samples. When the HT-hydrothermal time increases up to 24h, only perovskite structure can be detected. These changes reflect a reaction time dependent step-wise crystallization route, which can further be illustrated by the morphological evolution process. The SEM results (Fig. 1b) show that NBT-BNT-24h powder possesses nanocubic morphology and the average grain size is around 100 nm. The morphology of NBT-BNT-12h and NBT-BNT-18h are also shown in Fig. S2. It can be found that 12 h-hydrothermal reaction can only form small amorphous spherical particles, which grow gradually into large spheres with a diameter of
∼
500 nm when the hydrothermal time increases up to 18h. Intriguingly, the uniformly distributed regular nanocubes are detected after 24h-hydrothermal reaction. Therefore, it can be deduced that the regular nanocubes are crystallized by the dissolution of large particles with the reaction time longer than 18h. The elemental composition of NBT-BNT-24 h was analyzed by ICP. As shown in Table S1, the contents of Ni and Ba are determined to be 2.475% and 4.994% respectively, which are consistent with the designed composition (under the detection limits of the measured elements) (Mitrano et al., 2012). These results prove that the Ni2+ and Ba2+ have been successfully diffused into the NBT structure instead of being lost during the HT-hydrothermal process.

The crystal structure was analyzed by HR-TEM (Fig. 1c–d). The SEAD patterns of 100 nm NBT-BNT-24 h NCs in Fig. 1d shows only one set of regular crystal lattice, which proves that the NBT-BNT-24h belongs to single-crystalline structure. This diffraction pattern calibration results indicate that the cuboid particle's major face is parallel to the crystallographic (100) plane. The elemental distribution results for point and surface scanning in TEM images of NBT-BNT-24 h NCs exhibit the uniform distribution of Ni and Ba elements in the crystal lattice of NBT, which further confirms that the Ni and Ba elements were successfully diffused into the lattice structure during the HT-hydrothermal process (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation mechanism of single-crystalline nanocubes
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed mechanism underlying Ni ion induced single-crystalline nanocube formation is shown in Fig. 2 . For the NBT micro cubes, the formation mechanisms include in situ transformation or dissolution-recrystallization. When the alkalinity is high, the Bi2O3·nH2O and TiO2 H2O will be subsequently dissolved into Bi3+ and Ti(OH) x
(4−x) with close dissolution rates, as a result, Bi3+, Ti(OH) x
(4−x) and Na+ are homogeneously dispersed in the solution and then the microcubes of NBT are formed. However, in our work, the mediation of Ni2+ may influence the crystallization process of NBT-BT (Jiang et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2016). Since the nickel nitrates are more easily hydrolyzed to NiOH+ in the high alkalinity solution with 16 M NaOH, which has a strong interaction with the TiO2 H2O (Lu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019b). As a result, the NiOH + are adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 H2O and hinder the dissolution of TiO2 H2O (Fig. 2a&b). To reduce the surface free energy, the Bi3+, Na+ and Ba2+ dissolved in the solution are also adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 H2O. Then the localized adsorbed ions lead to spherical agglomerates (Fig. 2c), as shown by the morphology of NBT-BNT-18h. With prolonging the reaction time up to 24 h, the TiO2 H2O will sufficiently dissolve due to high alkaline condition and the NBT-BNT NCs are finally precipitated from the solution via chemical reaction (Fig. 2d), (Liu et al., 2004) as shown by the morphology of NBT-BNT-24h. The step-wise hydrothermal reaction equations can be formulated as follows:

Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of NBT-BNT-24 h NCs, NBT-BT prepared by HT-hydrothermal and NBT-BNT prepared by low temperature-hydrothermal (LT-hydrothermal). Obviously, the NBT-BNT-24 h NCs prepared by HT-hydrothermal possesses multi-stage visible and NIR light absorption edges at
∼
1300,
∼
920 and
∼
530 nm. In comparison, the NBT-BT sample has only one photo absorption edge at ∼400 nm and the NBT-BNT by LT-hydrothermal has negligible visible and NIR light absorption. The optical bandgaps of the NBT-BNT are determined through the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. 3b) (Gou et al., 2011). Through extrapolating the linear part of the plot between (αh
v
)2 vs. (h
v
) to the x-axis, the bandgap of NBT-BNT by HT-hydrothermal is calculated to be
∼
2.45eV with multigap states of
∼
1.50 eV and
∼
0.95 eV, while the bandgaps of HT-hydrothermal NBT-BT and LT-hydrothermal NBT-BNT are still as large as
∼
3.10 eV and
∼
2.96 eV, respectively. This result implies that the Ni+2 only can be sufficiently mediated into the structure of NBT-BT under severe conditions such as high temperature sintering or HT-hydrothermal process. Fig. S4 shows the bandgap model of NBT-BNT-24h nanocubes. The bandgap-engineering mechanism with Ni mediation has been well expounded in our previous work (Jia et al., 2019a). Since Ti4+ is partly substituted by the doped Ni2+, which constitute in the center position of oxygen octahedron, Ni 3d state splits due to asymmetric distance of Ni–O along Z direction, and this split energy state of Ni 3d locates in between VBM and CBM (Jia et al., 2019a). Thus, the successful introduction of Ni2+ into the lattice can endow the strong absorption of visible and NIR light.

Piezoresponse force microscope (PFM) was used to investigate the piezoelectric response properties of the NBT-BNT NCs. The applied bias was set from −40 V to 40 V. The topographic, amplitude, and phase image of the typical NBT-BNT NCs are shown in Fig. 4 (a-c). The NBT-BNT NCs can be clearly detected in the morphology image along with phase image. With the application of the DC voltage from −40 V to 40 V, the well-defined ferroelectric butterfly loop along with a phase change from 120° to −60° was obtained (Fig. 4d). By comparing the hysteresis loop with that of NBT-BNT nanospheres (Xiao et al., 2021), it is proved that the NBT-BNT single-crystalline NCs possess better piezoelectric response performance, which mainly comes from the single crystal structure with regular morphology.

To further enhance the visible light utilization efficiency and promote carrier's separation of NBT-BNT-24 h NCs, CQDs were used as a cocatalyst. As shown in Fig. S5, the nanospheres with a diameter of 10 nm can be detected on the surface of NBT-BNT NCs. The lattice spacing of the nanosphere is determined to be 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of CQDs (Shen et al., 2018). This result proves that the CQDs have been successfully loaded on the surface of NBT-BNT NCs. The XPS characterization of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs were used to study the chemical interaction between the CQDs and NBT-BNT NCs. The survey scan XPS spectrum of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs are showed in Fig. S6. The XPS peaks of C 1s, Ti 2p, Ni 2p and O 1s were coherently fitted by nearly Gaussian components. Fig. S6a shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s. The main peak at 284.4 eV is ascribed to the C–C bond with sp2 orbital (Di et al., 2015; Deng et al., 2021). The shoulder peak of the C 1s at the binding energy 288.4 eV is attributed to the C–O–Ti (Li et al., 2008), indicating interaction between Ti and C in the nanocomposites. The Ti 2p3/2 peak at 485.2 eV and Ti 2p1/2 peak at 464.0 eV also display a slight shift compared to Ti 2p peak of NBT-BNT sample (Xiao et al., 2021) (Fig. S6b), which indicates that the chemical environment of surface Ti4+ has been changed and the Ti–C chemical interaction exists in the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs samples. Besides, the XPS analyses of Ni 2p and O 1s can discover some structural information of NBT-BNT NCs. As shown in Fig. S6c, the Ni 2p peaks are detected, in which the main peak at 854.1 eV is ascribed to the Ni2+ in oxygen octahedral (BijuKhadar, 2002; Qiu et al., 2020), which further proves that the Ni2+ has successfully entered into the lattice during HT-hydrothermal process. The asymmetric O 1s peaks in Fig. S6d located at 529.6 eV and 531.4 eV are corresponding to the Ti–O bonds and oxygen vacancies respectively (Hsieh et al., 2007). The oxygen vacancies mainly come from the mediating of Ni2+ with low valence. The light absorption spectrum of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs is depicted in Fig. S7. The visible light absorption of NBT-BNT/CQDs at around 500–600 nm is stronger than that of the NBT-BNT NCs, which is attributed to the visible light absorption of the decorated CQDs (Jia et al., 2019a). The bandgap value of NBT-BNT NCs shows negligible changes after the decoration of the CQDs, indicating that the CQDs has no influence on the band structure of the NBT-BNT NCs. The step in the bandgap curves of NBT-BNT NCs@CQDs should arise from the photon excitation of CQDs (Jia et al., 2019a).

To further investigate the change of conductivity and the separation ability of photoinduced carriers in NBT-BNT NCs with the decoration of CQDs. The EIS, PL and PL fluorescent lifetime characterizations were measured for NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs respectively. The EIS curves of the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs shows a smaller radius than that of the NBT-BNT NCs (Fig. S8a), indicating that the decoration of CQDs can lower the charge transfer resistance (Dotan et al., 2011; Jo et al., 2012). Furthermore, PL measurements (Fig. S8b) reveal that the peak intensity of the NBT-BNT NCs is largely suppressed by decorating the CQDs. The weaker intensity represents lower recombination probability of photoexcited charge carriers (Xu et al., 2013), thus the photoexcited electrons can transfer to the CQDs more easily. Additionally, Fig. S8c presents the PL lifetime characterization results for pure NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs respectively. With fitting the PL fluorescent lifetime curves using the equitation of y = y

The NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs shows a longer electron lifetime of 7.68 ns compared to 4.39 ns for the pure single-crystalline nanocubes, which is in agreement with the results of the PL analysis.

The piezo-/photocatalytic performance of NBT-BNT NCs was comprehensively investigated using the organic dyes including RhB, MO and MB as the probe molecules. By measuring the dyes solution at each time interval during the catalysis, the absorption spectra of RhB solution degraded by NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under different condition are shown in Fig. S9, in which the typical RhB peak was confirmed at the wavelength of ∼552 nm. The RhB degradation efficiency is expressed by C/C0, where C0 is the normalized initial maximum absorbance at 552 nm, and C is the maximum absorbance of the solution at a specified time. As shown in Fig. 5 , there is less than 10% degradation of RhB for stirred solution with catalyst, implying that the adsorption of dyes on the NBT-BNT NCs or NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs surface is small. The RhB degradation results in Fig. 5a show that solitary NBT-BNT NCs possess much better visible light catalytic performance than that of visible light available BiFeO3 (Hengky et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2018; Mushtaq et al., 2020). It takes 80 min for NBT-BNT NCs to completely degrade the RhB under visible light, and the degradation time under ultrasound condition is 100 min. The multi-energy degradation time is as short as 40 min, indicating that the multi-energy catalytic efficiency of NBT-BNT NCs is much higher than that of NBT-BNT nanospheres (Xiao et al., 2021). Since CQDs can further improve the visible light utilization and promote carrier's separation, the catalytic efficiency for NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs is significantly enhanced. As shown in Fig. 5b, the RhB is completely degraded by NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs within only 8 min under visible light-ultrasound condition. Besides, it is found that RhB can be completely degraded within 3 min for NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under simulated sunlight-ultrasound condition (Fig. S10). With the application of visible light irradiation or ultrasound vibration alone, the piezoelectric catalytic performance of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs are also significantly enhanced. The RhB can be completely degraded within 15 min under visible light irradiation, while the CQDs itself could only degrade ∼35% of RhB within 30 min (Fig. 5b). And the RhB can be completely removed with a degradation time of 30 min under ultrasound vibration. The degradation kinetics are expressed by the pseudo-first order equation of ln(C/C0) = −k × t, where k is the reaction rate constant, as shown in Fig. 5c–d. Obviously, the k of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs for single and multi-energy catalysis are much larger than those of NBT-BNT NCs (Fig. S11). Especially, the rate constant of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under visible light–ultrasound condition is five times higher than that of NBT-BNT NCs (0.32218 min−1 vs. 0.06136 min−1). It is important to note that CQDs anchoring on the NBT-BNT NCs leads to larger surface area (Table S2), which increase solid-solution contact area providing more catalytic react sites for catalytic reaction (Li et al., 2019).NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs also show excellent degradation efficiency when comparing with various piezo-photocatalysts listed in Table S3. These results prove that CQDs can remarkably improve the visible light utilization efficiency and promote the separation of induced carriers. The cycling test conducted for 5 times (Fig. 5e) shows almost negligible decrement in catalytic efficiency, suggesting that the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs possess excellent catalytic stability for repeated irradiation and vibration. The catalytic degradation efficiency for MO and MB under visible light and ultrasound conditions are also measured in Fig. S12. The results show that cationic MB and anionic MO dyes can also be completely degraded by NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs with 30min and 50min respectively. This indicates that different kinds dyes can be efficiently decomposed by NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs. By comparing with the catalytic degradation efficiency of the reported works (Wang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019b; Lin et al., 2019; Qifeng et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019), the catalytic rates of NBT-BNT NCs are in a leading position, implying that the designed nano ferroelectrics in this work possess immense potential for the degradation of complex pollutants.

To further explore the catalytic mechanism of dyes degradation, quenching experiments for removing RhB were adopted to investigate the reactive intermediates responsible for piezo-photocatalytic dye degradation. For this purpose, the quenching agents of TBA (•OH scavenger), TEOA (h+ scavenger) and N2 flow (•O2− scavenger) were used (Zhong et al., 2020). The results of quenching experiments are depicted in Fig. 5f and Fig. S13. It is found that the introduction of TBA and N2 flow to the solution can pronouncedly retrogress the catalytic performance, while the addition of TEOA exerts a negligible influence on the multi-energy catalytic efficiency. Therefore, it can be concluded that the •O2− and •OH radicals are the dominant reactive species during the process of piezo-photocatalytic dyes degradation. In order to further confirm the formation of •O2− and •OH during the catalytic reaction, ESR spectrum for NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs with and without light irradiation were measured (Fig. S14). During the photocatalytic process, •O2− and •OH radicals are spin-trapped through 5,5-dimethy-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) individually. Thus, the ESR signals of •O2− and •OH radicals can be detected during the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. S14(a-b), no signals of •O2− and •OH radicals are observed in the dark condition. After being irradiated for 5 min, apparent signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2− can be detected, which directly prove that the •O2− and •OH radicals are generated by NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs during photocatalytic process. Fig. 6 a–b depict the H2 production rates by NBT-BNT NCs and NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under visible light/ultrasound/visible light-ultrasound condition. The H2 production rate of NBT-BNT NCs under visible light-ultrasound condition is 170.98
μ
mol g−1h−1, which even exceeds the H2 production rate of NBT nanospheres under sunlight-ultrasound condition (Zhao et al., 2020b). This excellent catalytic performance for ferroelectrics under visible light/ultrasound condition indicates that the strategy of Ni mediation through HT-hydrothermal has effectively enhanced the catalytic H2 generation rate. Fig. 6b shows the catalytic H2 generation results of NBT-BNT/CQDs under the same conditions. The H2 generation rate of NBT-BNT/CQDs can reach up to 350
μ
mol g−1h−1 under visible-ultrasound condition, which have increased 200% by that of solitary NBT-BNT NCs. Meanwhile, the performance of piezo-/photocatalytic H2 generation were also measured under visible light or ultrasound condition, with the values of 162.08 and 87.98
μ
mol g−1h−1, respectively, which means a synergistic effect between piezo-catalysis and photo-catalysis. The comparison in histograms for the average hydrogen production rates in Fig. 6c proves that the CQDs can effectively enhance the catalytic H2 generation performance. The simulated sunlight + ultrasound catalytic rate of NBT-BNT-24 h NCs can be as large as 747
μ
mol g−1h−1 (Fig. 6d). In order to exclude the contribution of cavitation bubbles to the H2 generation under the ultrasound condition, the piezocatalytic H2 production rate of TiO2 (P25) was also tested. As shown in Fig. S15, negligible H2 output was obtained within 4 h. Therefore, the excellent performance should be attributed to the NBT-BNT@CQDs nanocomposite catalysts. In the cyclic catalytic H2 generation test of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs, there is negligible decline for the H2 production rate after five consecutive multi-energy catalytic cycles, suggesting the excellent recyclability for the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under visible light-ultrasound condition. We have compared the catalytic H2 production rates of the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs with other typical ferroelectrics (Fig. 6e). (Liu et al., 2018, 2021; Yin et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2019b; Xie et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020a, 2020b; Huang et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021) It shows that the H2 production rate of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs under simulated sunlight-ultrasound condition is much higher than those of the reported ferroelectrics. Moreover, even when the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs is irradiated with visible light, the H2 production rate are superior to the reported visible light available ferroelectrics, which can be attributed to the narrow bandgap and the efficient separation of carriers of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs (Di et al., 2015).

The stability of the NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs during piezo-photocatalytic reaction is further confirmed by the XRD, UV–Vis–NIR absorption and TEM characterizations. In Fig. S16a, the XRD result shows a perovskite structure without impurity peaks, indicating no structure change of crystal occurred during the cyclic catalysis experiment. It is worth noting that no other signals of CQDs can be detected, which is due to the low CQDs content in the samples (Zhang et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2013). we also measured the light absorption spectrum of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs after cycling experiment (Fig. S16b). It shows that NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs after catalytic reaction still possesses strong absorption of visible light as that of pristine samples, indicating that the light absorption ability of the nanocomposites is stable during the multi-energy catalytic reaction. The TEM result can further support that the structure of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs possesses good stability during cycle catalytic reaction. In Fig. S16(c-d), the NBT-BNT NCs remain as a regular nanocubes structure, the little dots CQDs around the NBT-BNT NCs can be detected with an average diameter of 10 nm.

The piezo-photocatalytic dyes degradation and H2 generation results both show that the piezoelectric effect of NBT-BNT NCs can effectively enhance the photocatalytic efficiency. Hence, the mechanism of enhanced piezo-photocatalytic activity for NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs were further demonstrated here. We used the XPS SPECS system to determine the VB position which is at ∼1.56 eV (Xiao et al., 2021), combined with the band gap (∼2.45 eV) calculated by the UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra, the CB position of ∼ − 0.89 eV can be obtained. As shown in Fig. S17(a-b), we illustrate the band structure and potentials of NBT-BNT samples, in which the VBM position are more positive than that of O2/H2O and CBM much more negative than that of H+/H2O, this result indicates that the generated holes and electrons are energetically favorable for the reactions of various organic pollutants degradation and hydrogen evolution. Moreover, the position of gap state can be calculated as 0.11 V vs. RHE, which is still more positive than the potential of H+/H2O. As a result, the electrons that are photoexcited form the gap state to the CBM are also favorable for degrading the organic pollutants and producing hydrogen.

As shown in Fig. S17c, when the NBT-BNT NCs are irradiated by visible light, the electrons in the gap state can absorb enough energy and transit to the CBM. Besides, the electrons in VBM will transit to the gap states to supplement the electron loss. This process has been demonstrated in our previous work (Jia et al., 2019a). During photocatalytic process, most of the charge carriers will recombine and few carriers can transfer to the surface of catalysts, implying the separation and transporting efficiency of charge carriers are limited (Moscow et al., 2022).While during piezo-photocatalytic process for NBT-BNT NCs, the cavitation bubble in the ultrasound condition will apply the pressure on the NBT-BNT NCs and induce enough piezoelectric potential at the same time (Wu et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2019). The induced piezoelectric potential can effectively separate the charge carriers and impel them to drift to the opposite face of the nanocubes. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S17b, the polarization field induced by piezoelectric effect will lead to the band bending of VB, CB and gap states, which endow a larger potential energy for improving the mobility of holes and electrons (Goroff and Kleinman, 1963; Mohapatra et al., 2017; You et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). As a result, more charge carries can transfer to the surface of NBT-BNT NCs. After that, the electrons on the surface tend to transfer to CQDs and then the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs further promotes the separation of electrons and holes, leading to an enhanced piezo-photocatalytic activity. The accumulated electrons on CQDs can react with oxygen to form the activated •O2− or react with the hydrogen ions to produce H2, while the positive carriers can release the hydroxyl radicals •OH from the H2O molecules. Moreover, the exposed (100) planes can serve as the active facets with high surface energy to accelerate the catalytic reaction. Therefore, the piezo-photocatalytic performance of NBT-BNT NCs/CQDs is remarkably enhanced.

In summary, an effective strategy of high temperature hydrothermal method for Ni2+ mediated NBT-BT nanocubes was judiciously introduced, which simultaneously realized visible/near infrared light absorption and regular morphology controlling with specific exposed crystal plane. Based on the experimental results, the mechanism of Ni2+ for prompting the formation of single-crystalline nanocubes during high temperature hydrothermal process was revealed. CQDs were loaded on the NBT-BNT NCs to enhance the absorption of visible light and the separation ability of charge carriers. As a result, the catalytic RhB degradation time of NBT-BT based nanocomposites was reduced to merely 8 min and the hydrogen production rate reached ∼350 μmol g−1h−1 under visible light-ultrasonic condition, which is superior to those of conventional ferroelectric based piezo-photocatalytic. These tremendous improvements of catalytic performance can be attributed to the enhanced visible light absorption and effective carriers’ separation of the single-crystalline NBT-BNT nanocomposites. Considering the large group of bandgap-engineered ferroelectrics, this work can provide a rational strategy to modulate their morphology while improving the utilization ability of visible light, which can profoundly promote their application of pollutants degradation and clean energy production.

Yiping Guo and Hongyuan Xiao designed the experiments and coordinated the experimental work and associate discussions. Hongyuan Xiao and Jiahuan He conducted the experiments, analyzed the data and wrote the manuscript; Yiping Guo and Xunyu Lu provided assist in the revision of the paper. Feifei Wang provided assist in the analysis of PFM results.
